title: Lesson 01: Essential Greetings and 'To Be'
description: Start your Pashto journey with standard Kabul greetings, introductions, and the essential 'To Be' verb.
author: Poha Editorial

content.heading: The First Encounter
lines: In Pashto, the most common greeting is universal across the Islamic world: Salaam Alaykum [Sa-laam A-lay-koom] (Peace be upon you).
To ask 'How are you?' informally in the Kabul dialect, we say: Senga ye? [Sen-gah yay?]
To reply 'I am fine / good', say: Khah yam [Kh-ah yum]. Notice the 'kh' is a raspy sound from the back of the throat.

content.heading: The 'To Be' Verb (Present Tense)
lines: In Pashto's Subject-Object-Verb (SOV) structure, the verb ALWAYS goes at the end of the sentence.
Example: Ze khah yam [Zuh kh-ah yum] means 'I good am'.

content.text: Cultural Pro-Tip: When greeting someone, place your right hand over your heart. It shows deep respect and sincerity, instantly elevating you from a tourist to a guest.

content.question: If you want to say 'I am fine' in the Kabul dialect, what is the correct sentence order?
content.explanation: Pashto follows an SOV structure. 'Ze' (I) is the subject, 'khah' (fine) describes the state, and 'yam' (am) is the verb that must go at the end.
